perturbation.original_text: 11. Compliance with Securities Laws . Each Recipient acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may include material nonpublic information (within the meaning of the securities laws of the United States) with respect to Provider. Each Recipient agrees not to use and will cause its Representatives to agree not to use any Evaluation Material of Provider in violation of applicable securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: 19. Construction . The Parties have participated jointly in the negotiation and drafting of this Agreement. If an ambiguity or question of intent or interpretation arises, this Agreement shall be construed as if drafted jointly by the Parties and no presumption or burden of proof shall arise favoring or disfavoring either Party by virtue of the authorship at any of the provisions of this Agreement. 11. Compliance with Securities Laws . Each Recipient acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may include material nonpublic information (within the meaning of the securities laws of the United States) with respect to Provider. Each Recipient agrees not to use and will cause its Representatives to agree not to use any Evaluation Material of Provider in violation of applicable securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause on compliance with securities laws to the 'Construction' section makes it less prominent and harder to find, potentially weakening its enforceability. The original location is more appropriate for emphasizing the importance of adhering to securities laws, particularly regarding non-public information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the compliance clause to a less prominent section could be argued as a way to obscure the importance of complying with securities laws, potentially leading to violations of Rule 10b-5, which prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Placing such a critical disclosure in an unexpected location undermines its intended effect.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The change in the contract moves the 'Compliance with Securities Laws' clause to the 'Construction' section. This relocation weakens its prominence and discoverability, potentially violating Rule 10b-5, which prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices in securities transactions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce... (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud...' Placing the compliance clause in an unexpected location could be interpreted as a scheme to obscure its importance, thus creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the intended effect of ensuring compliance with securities laws.
